content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


September 8, 1993


Roger Lyon
Lyon and Carmel
1104 Palm Street
Post Office Box 922
San Luis Obispo, CA  93406

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-325

Dear Mr. Lyon:

     This is in response to your request for a confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on September 18, 1993, concerning
your responsibilities pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your letter
dated August 19, 1993, accurately reflects the advice I provided
you.

QUESTION

     1.  If a decision would have a material financial effect on a
person or entity which was a source of income of $250 or more to
the dependent child of a public official within 12 months of the
decision, is the public official prohibited from participating in
the decision?

     2.  Is the public official required to report the dependent
child's income on his or her statement of economic interest?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  A public official is not prohibited from participating in
a decision which may have a material financial effect on a person
or entity that was a source of income of $250 or more to the child
of the public official.

_______________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-325
Page 2




     2.  The public official is not required to report the child's
income on his or her statement of economic interest.

                              FACTS

     You are a designated employee for the Cambria Community
Services District.  You serve as district counsel, on a contract
basis, for the district.

     Over a year ago your dependent son purchased a heifer calf
from you for his 4-H Project.  He paid for the calf with money
which he earned on his own.  You stated neither you nor your wife
claim any interest in the heifer, nor do you have any expectation
or interest in any profit made from any sale of the heifer.

     The heifer was recently sold at an auction at the county fair
to the highest bidder.  The check was issued to your son from
Heritage Oaks Bank on the Mid State Fair's account.  The gross
purchase price was $1,400.  The net proceeds to your son from Mid
State Fair was $1,360.

     The successful bidder is an individual who in the past has
initiated litigation against the district concerning water rights
issues.  That litigation has been settled.  However, there are
ongoing issues and potential future disputes that may arise
between the district and the successful bidder.  It is foreseeable
that you will be called upon to provide legal advice to the
district that may have a financial effect on the bidder.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using the official's
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.  Section 87103 specifies that
an official has a financial interest if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Our File No. A-93-325
Page 3


(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent
for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103.

"Immediate family" is defined in Section 82029 as the
official's spouse and dependent children. A child is considered a
"dependent child" for purposes of the Act if the child is under 18
years of age and the elected official is entitled to claim the
dependent child as such for income tax purposes. (Tremlett Advice
Letter, No. I-89-386.). According to your letter dated
August 19, 1993, your dependent son is 14 years old. Therefore,
he is a member of your "immediate family" under this definition.

According to your letter, you and your wife do not claim any
interest in the heifer, nor do either of you expect, or have any
interest in, the profit made from the sale of the heifer.
However, as set forth in the last paragraph of Section 87103, an
investment held by a dependent child of a public official is
deemed to be an indirect investment.

You have indicated that the heifer is part of your son's
4-H Project. Under these facts, it appears that the heifer is

Our File No. A-93-325
Page 4


your son's asset,2 not an investment interest held by your son.3
Therefore, you do not have an indirect investment under Section
87103.

Your son has received $1,360 from the proceeds of the sale of
his heifer. We have advised that income received by a child of a
public official is not included in Section 82030, which defines a
"income" under the Act, and in Section 87103(c), and is therefore
not considered a financial interest of the official even if the
child is under 18 years of age and entitled to be claimed as a
dependent. (See, In re Cory (1976) 2 FPPC 48.; Ware Advice
Letter, No. A-86-259; Tremlett Advice Letter, supra.) Thus, the
proceeds from the sale of the heifer do not constitute disclosable
income to you.

Furthermore, absent some other disqualifying financial
interest, or a direct financial effect on you personally resulting
from the governmental decision, the proceeds from the sale are not
a basis for your disqualification. (Section 87103; Regulation
18702.1(a)(4).)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Wayne Ordos
Executive Director

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division




2 An asset includes property of all kinds, real and personal,
tangible and intangible. (Strauss Advice Letter, No. I-90-654.)

3 Please note this advice is limited to this set of facts. Under
the definition of "investment," an asset worth $1,000 or more may
be an investment. (Section 82034.) For example, we have advised
that where an official has an interest in a partnership which
breeds horses for a profit, an official has an investment under
the Act. (McAvoy Advice Letter, No. A-82-071.)

4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

decision,
is the public official prohibited from participating in

child’s income on his or her statement of economic interest?

CONCLUSION: S

A public official is not prohibited from participating in
a decision which may have a material financial effect on a person
or entity that was a source of income of $250 or more to the child
of the public official.
